220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

132	Both predicates are genuinely absent upstream, but neither is a free-form invention: each

146	`IsQuasicoherent`/`IsFinitePresentation` pattern verbatim and reusing

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
160	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
161	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
163	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
164	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
165	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
166	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
167	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
168	  %  the function
169	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
170	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
171	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
172	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
174	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
175	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
176	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
177	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
178	  \[
179	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
180	  \]
181	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
182	  the tensor product of sections
183	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
184	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
185	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
186	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
187	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
188	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
189	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
190	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
191	  polynomial ring).
192	\end{definition}

194	\begin{definition}
195	[Section graded module]
196	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
198	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
199	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
200	  % Polynomials" (read from
201	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
204	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
205	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
206	  %  the function
207	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
208	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
209	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
210	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
212	  I.7.}
213	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
214	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
215	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
216	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
217	  \[
218	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
219	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
220	  \]
221	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
222	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
223	  by the section tensor product
224	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
225	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
226	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
227	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
228	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).

231	\begin{lemma}
232	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
233	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
235	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
236	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
237	  % Polynomials" (read from
238	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
239	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
240	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
241	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
242	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
243	  %  the function
244	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
245	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
246	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
247	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
248	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
249	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
250	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
251	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
252	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
253	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
254	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
255	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
256	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
257	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{proof}
261	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
262	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
263	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
264	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
265	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
266	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
267	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
268	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
269	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
270	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
271	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
272	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
273	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
274	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
275	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
276	\end{proof}

278	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
279	  graded series]
280	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
281	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
282	  \mathlibok
283	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
284	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
285	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
286	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
287	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
288	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
289	  equals \(h(n)\):
290	  \[
291	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
292	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
293	  \]
294	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
295	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
296	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
297	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
298	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
299	\end{lemma}

301	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
302	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
304	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
305	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
306	  % Polynomials" (read from
307	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
310	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
311	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
312	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
313	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
314	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
315	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
316	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
317	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
318	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
319	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
320	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
321	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
322	  such that for all \(n > N\)
323	  \[
324	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
325	  \]
326	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
327	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
328	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
329	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
330	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
331	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
332	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
333	
334	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
335	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
336	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
337	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
338	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
339	  rational series from the module.
340	\end{lemma}
341	
342	\begin{proof}
343	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
344	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
345	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
346	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
347	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
348	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
349	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
350	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
351	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
352	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
353	  graded modules
354	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
355	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
356	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
357	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
358	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
359	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
360	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
361	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
362	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
363	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
364	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
368	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
370	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
371	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
372	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
373	  % Polynomials" (read from
374	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
375	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
376	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
377	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
378	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
379	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
380	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
381	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
382	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
383	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
384	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
385	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
386	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
387	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
388	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
389	  \[
390	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
391	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
392	  \]
393	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
394	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
395	\end{theorem}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
399	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
400	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
401	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
402	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
403	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
404	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
405	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
406	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
407	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
408	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
409	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
410	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
411	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
412	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
413	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
414	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
415	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
416	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
417	\end{proof}

469	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
470	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
471	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
472	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
473	  \[
474	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
475	  \]
476	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
477	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
478	  \begin{enumerate}
479	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
480	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
481	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
482	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
483	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
484	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
485	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
486	      \(\Phi\).

489	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
490	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
491	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
492	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
493	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
494	\end{definition}

496	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}

502	linear algebra), we package the construction here.

504	\begin{definition}\leanok
505	[Grassmannian scheme]
506	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}

541	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
542	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
543	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor

551	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
552	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
553	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
554	
555	  Structurally \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as this Quot functor
556	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) of the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
557	  \(\Phi = d\) -- not as a second, independently-built functor. Fixing the
558	  definition this way makes the identity
559	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) hold by
560	  construction, so the structural correspondence is preserved automatically
561	  when the underlying \(\Quot\) construction is later filled in, and the
562	  Grassmannian inherits its functoriality and equivalence relation from the
563	  Quot functor rather than restating them.
564	\end{definition}

634	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
635	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
636	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
637	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
638	  equipped with a tautological quotient
639	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
640	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
641	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding

647	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
648	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
649	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
650	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
651	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total

654	\begin{proof}
655	  
656	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
657	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
658	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
659	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
660	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
661	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
662	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
663	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
664	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
665	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
666	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
667	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
668	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
669	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
670	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
671	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
672	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
673	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
674	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
675	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
676	
677	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
678	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
679	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
680	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
681	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
682	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
683	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
684	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
685	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
686	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
687	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
688	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
689	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
690	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
691	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
692	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
693	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
694	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
695	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
696	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
697	\end{proof}

700	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}

704	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
705	\begin{itemize}

708	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,

711	    project-side substrate -- not constructed in this chapter.
712	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
713	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
714	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
715	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
716	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
717	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited

392	\bigskip
393	
394	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and $\Quot_{E/X/S}$}
395	
396	\medskip
397	
398	The above functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ and $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
399	admit the following simple generalisations. 
400	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $X\to S$ be a finite type
401	scheme over it. Let $E$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$. 
402	Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
403	over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$ 
404	parametrised by $T$} will mean 
405	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
406	
407	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$ 
408	such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$ 
409	and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with 
410	
411	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
412	$q:E_T  \to \F$ where $E_T$ is the pull-back of $E$ under
413	the projection $X_T \to X$.
414	
415	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $T$ will 
416	be regarded as equivalent if $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$), 
417	and $\langle \F, q \rangle$ will denote 
418	an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
419	by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact, 
420	the pull-back of $\langle \F, q \rangle$ under an $S$-morphism $T'\to T$ is
421	well-defined, which gives
422	a set-valued contravariant functor 
423	$\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ under which   
424	$$T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}$$
425	
426	When $E = {\mathcal O}_X$, the functor $\Quot_{{\mathcal O}_X/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ 
427	associates to $T$ the set of all closed subschemes $Y\subset X_T$
428	that are proper and flat over $T$. We denote this functor by $\Hilb_{X/S}$.
429	
430	Note in particular that we have
431	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n} = \Hilb_{\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z} \mbox{ and }
432	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}} = 
433	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}}/\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z}$$ 
434	
435	
436	It is clear that the functors $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ and $\Hilb_{X/S}$
437	satisfy faithfully flat descent, 
438	so it makes sense to pose the question of their
439	representability. 
440	
441	
442	
443	\medskip
444	
445	
446	\bigskip
447	
448	\centerline{\large\bf Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials}
449	
450	\medskip
451	
452	
453	Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
454	a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
455	Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on 
456	$X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
457	the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
458	the function 
459	$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) = 
460	\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
461	where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the 
462	coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
463	is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
464	case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
465	
466	
467	
468	Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
469	and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
470	be any coherent sheaf on 
471	$X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
472	we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$ 
473	which is the Hilbert polynomial of
474	the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
475	calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$. 
476	If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$ 
477	from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is 
478	known to be locally constant on $S$. 
479	
480	
481	This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
482	as a co-product 
483	$$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
484	where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor 
485	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
486	to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of 
487	families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the 
488	Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
489	using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$. Correspondingly,
490	the representing scheme  $\quot_{E/X/S}$, when it exists, naturally decomposes
491	as a co-product
492	$$\quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
493	
494	\textbf{Note } 
495	We will generally take $X$ to be (quasi-)projective over $S$,
496	and $L$ to be a relatively very ample line bundle. Then indeed 
497	the Hilbert and Quot functors are representable by schemes,
498	but not in general.
499	
500	
501	
502	\medskip
503	
504	
505	\bigskip
506	
507	\centerline{\large\bf Elementary Examples, Exercises}
508	
509

546	\textbf{(2) } \textbf{Grassmannian as a Quot scheme } 
547	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, an explicit construction
548	the Grassmannian scheme
549	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
550	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
551	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
552	has been given at the end of this section. A proof of the 
553	properness of $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is given there, 
554	together with a closed embedding $\grass(r,d)\hra 
555	\PP(\pi_*\det\U) = \PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m={r \choose d} - 1$.
556	
557	Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient 
558	$u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the 
559	contravariant functor 
560	$$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$ 
561	from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
562	set of all equivalence classes 
563	$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
564	where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
565	Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$ 
566	exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$.
567	
568	
569	\textbf{Grassmannian of a vector bundle} 
570	Show that for any ring
571	$A$, the action of the group $GL_r(A)$ on the free module $\oplus^r A$ 
572	induces an action of $GL_r(A)$ on 
573	the set $\Grass(r,d)(A)$, such that for any ring homomorphism $A\to B$,
574	the set-map $\Grass(r,d)(A)\to\Grass(r,d)(B)$ is equivariant with respect
575	to the group homomorphism $GL_r(A) \to GL_r(B)$. (In schematic terms,
576	this means we have an action of the group-scheme $GL_{r,\Z}$ on 
577	$\grass(r,d)$.) 
578	
579	Using the above show that, more generally, if $S$ is a scheme and 
580	$E$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_S$-module of rank $r$, 
581	the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$ 
582	on all $S$-schemes which 
583	by definition associates to any $T$ 
584	the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients  
585	$q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,
586	is representable. The representing scheme
587	is denoted by $\grass(E,d)$ and is called the rank $d$ 
588	relative Grassmannian of $E$ over $S$.
589	It parametrises a universal quotient $\pi^*E \to \F$
590	where $\pi : \grass(E,d) \to S$ is the projection.
591	Show that the determinant line bundle $\bigwedge^d \F$ on
592	$\grass(E,d)$ is relatively very ample over $S$, 
593	and it gives a closed embedding
594	$\grass(E,d) \hra \PP(\pi_* \bigwedge^d \F) \subset \PP(\bigwedge^d E)$. 
595	(The properness of the embedding follows from the 
596	properness of $\pi : \grass(E,d) \to S$, which follows locally
597	over $S$ by base-change from  properness of 
598	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$ -- see Exercise \textbf{(5)}
599	or \textbf{(7)} below.)
600	
601	
602	\textbf{Grassmannian of a coherent sheaf} 
603	If $E$ is a coherent sheaf on $S$, not necessarily locally free, 
604	then by definition the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$ 
605	on all $S$-schemes associates to any $T$ 
606	the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients  
607	$q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free on $T$ of rank $d$.

\section{Support and freeness predicates}
\label{sec:quot_predicates}

The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
geometric conditions that are not yet primitive in the underlying sheaf-of-modules
formalism: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support proper over the
base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally free of a fixed
rank}. This section encodes both predicates. Coherence itself is carried, as in
\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule} and the generic-flatness layer, by the
two object predicates \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\) and
\(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}\) already available on a sheaf of modules; the
new content here is the support and the rank predicates.

\begin{definition}
[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
  %  be any coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
  project-built primitive, see the note below.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on \(X\) ---
  an object of the category \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
  which in the formalism is \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf})\).
  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
  \[
    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \in X.\mathrm{IdealSheafData},
    \qquad
    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F}) \;=\;
    \mathrm{ofIdeals}\Bigl( U \;\longmapsto\;
    \mathrm{Ann}_{\Gamma(X,U)}\,\Gamma(\mathcal{F},U) \Bigr),
  \]
  which assigns to each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) the annihilator ideal of the
  module of sections \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}, U)\) over the ring \(\Gamma(X, U)\).
  This is the schematic incarnation of the support of \(\mathcal{F}\): its
  vanishing locus is exactly where the stalks of \(\mathcal{F}\) are nonzero.

  \emph{Note (project-built primitive).} Mathlib carries no support or annihilator
  of a \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\) / \(\mathrm{Scheme.Modules}\); the only support
  primitives in algebraic geometry are at the level of ideal sheaves
  (\(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.support}\)) and morphism kernels
  (\(\mathrm{Hom.ker}\)). The definition above is therefore introduced here,
  mirroring \(\mathrm{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.ker}\)
  (\texttt{IdealSheaf/Basic.lean}), which is built as
  \(\mathrm{ofIdeals}\,(U \mapsto \mathrm{RingHom.ker}\,(f.\mathrm{app}\,U))\) by
  the same \(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.ofIdeals}\) constructor
  (\texttt{IdealSheaf/Basic.lean}); the affine-local datum is the ring-theoretic
  \(\mathrm{Module.annihilator}\) (\texttt{RingTheory/Ideal/Colon.lean}). It is
  \emph{not} marked as supplied by Mathlib.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
  \label{def:schematic_support}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
  %  be any coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme cut out by
  its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
  \[
    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
    \;=\; \bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme},
  \]
  equipped with the canonical closed immersion
  \(\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme}\iota :
  \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X\). This uses the
  closed-subscheme-of-an-ideal-sheaf machinery
  (\(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.subscheme}\) and \(\mathrm{subscheme}\iota\),
  \texttt{IdealSheaf/Subscheme.lean}), whose inclusion is automatically a
  preimmersion and quasi-compact, hence a closed immersion onto the support of
  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\). It realises Nitsure's ``schematic support of
  \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as an honest scheme over \(X\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
  The class of \emph{proper} morphisms of schemes is the morphism property
  \[
    \mathrm{IsProper}
    \;=\; (\mathrm{IsSeparated} \,\sqcap\, \mathrm{UniversallyClosed})
    \,\sqcap\, \mathrm{LocallyOfFiniteType},
  \]
  i.e.\ a morphism \(f : X \to S\) is proper iff it is separated, universally
  closed, and locally of finite type. As a \(\mathrm{MorphismProperty}\) it is
  stable under isomorphism, composition, and -- crucially for the Quot functor's
  pullback action -- \emph{stable under base change}
  (\(\mathrm{IsProper.isStableUnderBaseChange}\)). Consequently the
  base-change clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by
  base-change'') is discharged for free for the proper-support condition below.
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}
[Proper support over the base]
  \label{def:has_proper_support}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
  \uses{def:schematic_support, lem:isProper_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
  % + L418-L419).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
  %  [...]
  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite of the canonical closed immersion
  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) with \(f\) is proper:
  \[
    \mathrm{HasProperSupport}\,f\,\mathcal{F}
    \;:\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{IsProper}\Bigl(
      \bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr).\mathrm{subscheme}\iota \;\circ\; f
    \Bigr),
  \]
  where \(\mathrm{IsProper}\) is the Mathlib morphism property of
  \cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}. Reusing \(\mathrm{IsProper}\) rather than
  re-bundling separatedness, universal closedness, and finite type by hand makes
  the base-change stability of proper support immediate, which is precisely what
  the well-definedness of the Quot functor's pullback action requires.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
  % + L584-L585).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
  %  [...]
  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules and let \(r \in \mathbb{N}\). We say
  \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank \(r\)} when there is a witness
  exhibiting \(\mathcal{M}\), locally on an open cover, as the free sheaf of
  modules on the finite index set \(\{1, \dots, r\}\):
  \[
    \mathrm{IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\,\mathcal{M}\,r
    \;:\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(\text{local-trivialisation data of }\mathcal{M}
      \text{ against } \mathrm{free}(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\bigr),
  \]
  the data consisting of an open cover of the base together with, on each member,
  an isomorphism of the restriction of \(\mathcal{M}\) with
  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.free}\,(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\) (the coproduct of \(r\)
  copies of the unit module sheaf).

  \emph{Encoding note.} The rank is carried as the parameter \(r\) of the local
  model \(\mathrm{free}(\mathrm{Fin}\,r)\); it is \emph{not} a rank-agnostic
  local-freeness predicate conjoined with a sheaf-level
  \(\mathrm{rankAtStalk} = r\) condition, because no sheaf-level
  \(\mathrm{rankAtStalk}\) exists in Mathlib (it is a ring-level invariant
  \(\mathrm{Module.rankAtStalk}\) on \(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum}\)). The shape
  parallels the existing object predicates on a sheaf of modules ---
  \(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{M} := \mathrm{Nonempty}\,
  (\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,\mathcal{M})\) and
  \(\mathrm{IsFinitePresentation}\) (\texttt{Sheaf/Quasicoherent.lean}),
  \(\mathrm{IsFiniteType}\) (\texttt{Sheaf/Generators.lean}) --- and is built on
  the free-sheaf machinery \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.free}\),
  \(\mathrm{freeFunctor}\), and \(\mathrm{mapFree}\)
  (\texttt{Sheaf/Free.lean}). If the trivialisation isomorphisms are needed as
  data downstream, the predicate splits into a data carrier and its
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\) propositional truncation, exactly as
  \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\) / \(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\) do. It is
  \emph{not} marked as supplied by Mathlib.
\end{definition}

\label{sec:quot_functor}
